original_hypothesis.title: Interest Rate Index uint128 Overflow Permanently Bricks Asset Operations
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Supply and borrow indices are stored as uint128 and updated via SafeCast.toUint128(). Starting from 1e27 (RAY), the maximum growth factor before overflow is ~3.4e11x. While this seems large, a misconfigured interest rate model returning extremely high rates, or a prolonged period without any interaction on a high-rate asset, could cause the index to overflow. Once this happens, all operations on the asset (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay) permanently revert.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The overflow is irreversible - there is no admin recovery mechanism. The asset becomes permanently bricked, locking all user funds. Even legitimate rate models could produce dangerously high indices over long time horizons without user interaction to reset the timestamp.
deployment_assumptions: Interest rate model returns extremely high rates, or asset goes without interaction for extended period
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: InterestLogic.sol uses .toUint128() for both supplyIndex and borrowIndex; deployed rate models have base rates up to 10% with slope up to 100%

proof_of_signal.pattern: assetData.supplyIndex = nextSupplyIndex.toUint128() and groupData.borrowIndex = nextBorrowIndex.toUint128() — SafeCast reverts on overflow with no recovery

preconditions: Interest rate model returns very high rates (>100% APY compounded)
No interaction with the asset for an extended period

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: toUint128() will revert when index exceeds ~3.4e38; starting from 1e27, this allows ~3.4e11x growth
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set up conditions where no one interacts with asset while high rates compound; or admin sets malicious rate model
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; permanent DoS locks all user funds in the asset

economic_questions: At maximum deployed rate (100% slope), how long until overflow?
Can the rate model be manipulated to return rates that cause faster overflow?
What are the time bounds for different rate scenarios?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum time-to-overflow for deployed rate model parameters
Test what happens when toUint128 reverts in updateInterestIndexs

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Depends on rate model; potentially years at normal rates, much less with extreme rates

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When asset has high utilization (high rates) and no activity
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All deposited and borrowed funds for the asset permanently frozen

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All depositors and borrowers of the affected asset
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of their deposited/borrowed amount in that asset
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total supply + total borrow for the asset

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Max rate = baseRate(10%) + slope(100%) at 100% utilization = 110% APY compound
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: At 110% APY: 1.1^n * 1e27 > 3.4e38 → n > log(3.4e11)/log(1.1) ≈ 279 years (safe at normal rates)

validation_result.mechanism: uint128 index overflow with no recovery
validation_result.impact_summary: Supply and borrow indices stored as uint128 will permanently brick an asset if they overflow. Under deployed rate models (~115% APY max), overflow takes ~190+ years, but a misconfigured or malicious rate model with no upper-bound validation could trigger overflow much sooner.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that InterestLogic.sol stores supplyIndex and borrowIndex as uint128 and uses SafeCast.toUint128() which reverts on overflow. Starting from 1e27 (RAY), the maximum growth factor before overflow is ~3.4e11x. The code has three compounding issues: (1) No validation on rate model return values beyond uint128 max (~3.4e38 in RAY, representing an absurdly high rate), (2) No cap on how large indices can grow, and (3) No recovery mechanism if overflow occurs. Under deployed DefaultInterestRateModel parameters (max ~115% APY at full utilization), overflow would take ~190 years. However, the admin can set arbitrary rate models via setAssetLendingRate() with no bounds checking on the rate values the model produces. A severely misconfigured model returning, say, 10000% APY could cause overflow in ~5.7 years without interaction. The defect is real but its practical impact depends entirely on rate model configuration.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is verified. InterestLogic.sol lines ~163-166 and ~176-181 show the toUint128() casts. The _updateSupplyIndex function at line ~163 does `assetData.supplyIndex = nextSupplyIndex.toUint128()` and _updateBorrowIndex at line ~181 does `groupData.borrowIndex = nextBorrowIndex.toUint128()`. Both will revert if the computed index exceeds type(uint128).max. The updateInterestIndexs function is called by virtually every state-changing operation (deposits, withdrawals, borrows, repays), so a revert here bricks the entire asset.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: SafeCast.toUint128() reverts if nextSupplyIndex exceeds type(uint128).max. Since supplyIndex only increases and there's no recovery mechanism, once the rate accumulation pushes the index past uint128 max, all operations on the asset permanently revert.

Same issue as supplyIndex. The borrowIndex uses compounded interest which grows faster. SafeCast.toUint128() will permanently revert once the accumulated compound interest pushes the index past uint128 max.

The borrow rate is fetched from an external rate model with no validation on the magnitude of the returned value (beyond uint128 max). A malicious or misconfigured rate model can return extremely high rates that accelerate index overflow.

execution_path_analysis.entry_point: Any state-changing operation (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay) via Proxy -> PoolManager.dispatch() -> module
calling_sequence: User calls any lending operation (e.g., crossBorrowERC20)
updateInterestIndexs also calls _updateBorrowIndex for each group
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerable path is triggered by ANY interaction with an asset after the accumulated interest pushes the index past uint128 max. Since updateInterestIndexs is called at the start of every operation, and interest accumulates based on the time gap since lastUpdateTimestamp, a long idle period with high rates causes the compounded interest multiplier to be very large. When multiplied by the current index, the result exceeds uint128 max, and toUint128() reverts. All subsequent calls to updateInterestIndexs revert, permanently freezing the asset.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Interest rate model returns extremely high rates, or asset goes without interaction for extended period
config_validation.deployment_evidence: InitConfigPool.s.sol shows deployed rate models: irmHigh has baseRate=10%, slope1=5%, slope2=100%, optimal=65%. Maximum effective rate at 100% utilization is approximately 115% APY. At this rate, overflow takes ~190 years. However, setAssetLendingRate allows admin to change rate models at any time with no bounds on model output. The scripts do not show any extreme rate models being deployed.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Under deployed rate model parameters (max ~115% APY), the overflow requires ~190 years without asset interaction — practically infeasible. However, the admin can set arbitrary rate models via Configurator.setAssetLendingRate(). A compromised or careless admin could deploy a rate model returning rates of 10000%+ APY, reducing overflow time to single-digit years. Additionally, if an asset has outstanding borrows but no interaction for a very long period at elevated rates, the compounded interest in _updateBorrowIndex could reach overflow. The attack is realistic only in scenarios involving admin misconfiguration or compromise, not under normal operation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If triggered, the impact is CRITICAL: all deposits, withdrawals, borrows, and repays for the affected asset permanently revert. There is no admin recovery mechanism — no function to reset indices or bypass the overflow. All user funds deposited as that asset are permanently locked. The severity is mitigated by the extreme conditions required under deployed configurations (~190+ years), but the absence of a recovery mechanism and rate bounds makes it a genuine code defect.
recommended_mitigations: Add a maximum rate cap in updateInterestRates: require(vars.nextGroupBorrowRate <= MAX_ALLOWED_RATE) before storing
Consider using uint256 for indices instead of uint128 to eliminate the overflow risk entirely
Add an emergency admin function to reset/adjust indices if overflow occurs
Add a maximum time delta check in calculateCompoundedInterest to limit the gap between updates
Add monitoring/alerts for index growth rate approaching dangerous levels
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a malicious IInterestRateModel that returns a very high rate (e.g., 1e30 in RAY). 2. As pool admin, set this rate model for an asset group via setAssetLendingRate. 3. Deposit and borrow a small amount to create outstanding debt. 4. Wait for some time (or warp block.timestamp in a test). 5. Attempt any operation on the asset — it should revert with SafeCast overflow. 6. Verify that no subsequent operations succeed on that asset.
uncertainties: The exact rate model implementations used in production are not in scope, so the maximum realistic rate is estimated from deployment script parameters
The binomial approximation in calculateCompoundedInterest slightly underestimates true compound interest, which marginally extends time-to-overflow but doesn't change the fundamental issue
Whether governance includes rate model auditing processes that would catch extreme values before deployment
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with various rate model outputs to find minimum rate that causes overflow within practical timeframes (1-10 years)
Test the exact revert behavior when toUint128 fails in updateInterestIndexs — confirm all operations are indeed bricked
Test whether any admin function can bypass updateInterestIndexs to recover the asset
Test with maximum legitimate time gaps between interactions at various rate levels
